summary: A methodology for building software-as-a-service applications that are portable, cloud-ready, continuously deployable, and scalable without architectural change.

blocks.content: The Twelve-Factor App

source_span.quote: The Twelve-Factor App

blocks.content: In the modern era, software is commonly delivered as a service: called web apps, or software-as-a-service.

source_span.quote: In the modern era, software is commonly delivered as a service: called web apps, or software-as-a-service.

blocks.content: The twelve-factor app is a methodology for building software-as-a-service apps that:

source_span.quote: The twelve-factor app is a methodology for building software-as-a-service apps that:

blocks.content: The twelve-factor methodology can be applied to apps written in any programming language, and which use any combination of backing services (database, queue, memory cache, etc).

source_span.quote: The twelve-factor methodology can be applied to apps written in any programming language, and which use any combination of backing services (database, queue, memory cache, etc).

blocks.content: The contributors to this document have been directly involved in the development and deployment of hundreds of apps, and indirectly witnessed the development, operation, and scaling of hundreds of thousands of apps via our work on the Heroku platform.

source_span.quote: The contributors to this document have been directly involved in the development and deployment of hundreds of apps, and indirectly witnessed the development, operation, and scaling of hundreds of thousands of apps via our work on the Heroku platform.

blocks.content: This document synthesizes all of our experience and observations on a wide variety of software-as-a-service apps in the wild. It is a triangulation on ideal practices for app development, paying particular attention to the dynamics of the organic growth of an app over time, the dynamics of collaboration between developers working on the app's codebase, and avoiding the cost of software erosion.

source_span.quote: This document synthesizes all of our experience and observations on a wide variety of software-as-a-service apps in the wild. It is a triangulation on ideal practices for app development, paying particular attention to the dynamics of the organic growth of an app over time, the dynamics of collaboration between developers working on the app's codebase, and avoiding the cost of software erosion.

blocks.content: Our motivation is to raise awareness of some systemic problems we've seen in modern application development, to provide a shared vocabulary for discussing those problems, and to offer a set of broad conceptual solutions to those problems with accompanying terminology. The format is inspired by Martin Fowler's books Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture and Refactoring.

source_span.quote: Our motivation is to raise awareness of some systemic problems we've seen in modern application development, to provide a shared vocabulary for discussing those problems, and to offer a set of broad conceptual solutions to those problems with accompanying terminology. The format is inspired by Martin Fowler's books Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture and Refactoring.

blocks.content: Any developer building applications which run as a service. Ops engineers who deploy or manage such applications.

source_span.quote: Any developer building applications which run as a service. Ops engineers who deploy or manage such applications.

blocks.content: I. Codebase — One codebase tracked in revision control, many deploys

source_span.quote: I. Codebase — One codebase tracked in revision control, many deploys

blocks.content: II. Dependencies — Explicitly declare and isolate dependencies

source_span.quote: II. Dependencies — Explicitly declare and isolate dependencies

blocks.content: III. Config — Store config in the environment

source_span.quote: III. Config — Store config in the environment

blocks.content: IV. Backing services — Treat backing services as attached resources

source_span.quote: IV. Backing services — Treat backing services as attached resources

blocks.content: V. Build, release, run — Strictly separate build and run stages

source_span.quote: V. Build, release, run — Strictly separate build and run stages

blocks.content: VI. Processes — Execute the app as one or more stateless processes

source_span.quote: VI. Processes — Execute the app as one or more stateless processes

blocks.content: VII. Port binding — Export services via port binding

source_span.quote: VII. Port binding — Export services via port binding

blocks.content: VIII. Concurrency — Scale out via the process model

source_span.quote: VIII. Concurrency — Scale out via the process model

blocks.content: IX. Disposability — Maximize robustness with fast startup and graceful shutdown

source_span.quote: IX. Disposability — Maximize robustness with fast startup and graceful shutdown

blocks.content: X. Dev/prod parity — Keep development, staging, and production as similar as possible

source_span.quote: X. Dev/prod parity — Keep development, staging, and production as similar as possible

blocks.content: XI. Logs — Treat logs as event streams

source_span.quote: XI. Logs — Treat logs as event streams

blocks.content: XII. Admin processes — Run admin/management tasks as one-off processes

source_span.quote: XII. Admin processes — Run admin/management tasks as one-off processes
